---
title: "WMaxTwin Doppler/Nason Cross-Validation in R"
author: "Brani Vidakovic, Youngseo Cho, and V. Roshan Joseph"
output:
  html_notebook:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE, fig.width = 8, fig.height = 4.8)
```

# Purpose

This notebook is an annotated R version of Example II in the WMaxTwin paper.
The example revisits Nason-type wavelet cross-validation for the Doppler signal
and compares:

1. the noisy signal,
2. levelwise SURE thresholding,
3. Nason's even/odd cross-validation benchmark,
4. a location-only MaxTwin split with a fixed threshold family,
5. WMaxTwin with a multiscale split and a high-frequency ramp threshold family,
6. an oracle ramp benchmark that uses the true signal and is not available in practice.

The important methodological point is not that WMaxTwin simply replaces Nason's
even/odd split.  For a single gridded signal, an arbitrary MaxTwin or WMaxTwin
mask is generally not a dyadic subgrid.  Therefore one should not apply an
ordinary DWT to the retained observations.  The fit used here is a masked
wavelet penalized least-squares fit on the original grid.

# Mathematical setup

The Doppler signal is

$$
f_0(t)=\{t(1-t)\}^{1/2}\sin\left({2\pi\,1.05\over t+0.05}\right),
\qquad 0<t<1.
$$

The signal is centered and rescaled so that the signal-to-noise ratio is
$SNR=7$ when $\sigma=1$.  The observations are

$$
y_i=f(t_i)+\epsilon_i,
\qquad
\epsilon_i\sim N(0,\sigma^2),
\qquad
 t_i={i-1/2\over n}.
$$

We use $n=1024$, Symmlet 4, and wavelet levels $j=3,\ldots,9$.

# What the mask means

For one MaxTwin or WMaxTwin split, each original grid location $t_i$ is assigned
to one of two halves.  For a given masked fit, the training indicator is

$$
m_i=
\begin{cases}
1, & \hbox{if } y_i \hbox{ is used for training},\\
0, & \hbox{if } y_i \hbox{ is held out for validation}.
\end{cases}
$$

The masked wavelet fit is

$$
\widehat d_\lambda
=
\arg\min_d
\left[
{1\over 2}\sum_{i=1}^n m_i\{y_i-(W'd)_i\}^2
+
\sum_{j=3}^{9}\lambda_j\sum_k |d_{jk}|
\right].
$$

The scaling coefficients at level 3 are not penalized.  The estimator is solved
by FISTA using only applications of $W$ and $W'$.  This is a row-restricted
wavelet regression problem on the original grid.  It is not a DWT of the retained
observations treated as a shorter regular signal.

# WMaxTwin feature geometry

The WMaxTwin feature vector at grid location $t_i$ is

$$
u_i=
\left(t_i,
\sqrt{\omega_3}q_i^{(3)},
\sqrt{\omega_4}q_i^{(4)},
\ldots,
\sqrt{\omega_9}q_i^{(9)}
\right),
$$

where $q_i^{(j)}$ is a standardized local activity measure from the level $j$
wavelet detail contribution.  The paper weights are

| Level $j$ | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $\omega_j$ | 0.015 | 0.036 | 0.074 | 0.124 | 0.182 | 0.248 | 0.321 |

Thus the WMaxTwin geometry gives most of the multiscale emphasis to the active
high-frequency levels $7,8,9$, without removing the lower levels.

# R implementation

The file `wmaxtwin_doppler_nason_cv_R.R` contains a fully self-contained R
implementation of the periodic Symmlet 4 DWT/IDWT, masked FISTA fitting,
Nason/MaxTwin/WMaxTwin splits, cross-validation, and plotting.  No wavelet R
package is required.

```{r source-code}
RUN_MAIN <- FALSE
source("wmaxtwin_doppler_nason_cv_R.R")
```

# Check the WMaxTwin feature weights

```{r weights}
J <- as.integer(log2(N))
wmaxtwin_scale_weights(J0, J, mode = WEIGHT_MODE)
```

# Run the simulation

The default run uses 18 Monte Carlo replications, matching the paper-scale
experiment.  For a quick GitHub test, set `NREP <- 2` or `NREP <- 3` before
calling `run_simulation()`.

```{r run-simulation}
result <- run_simulation(
  n = N, snr = SNR, sigma = SIGMA, wavelet = WAVELET,
  j0 = J0, nrep = NREP, seed = SEED, n_iter = N_ITER,
  weight_mode = WEIGHT_MODE, output_dir = OUTPUT_DIR
)
result$summary
```

# Figures

The R script writes figures to `outputs_R/`.  They are shown below after the
simulation has been run.

```{r figure-one-rep, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(OUTPUT_DIR, "fig01_one_replication.png"))
```

```{r figure-weights, out.width="75%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(OUTPUT_DIR, "fig02_feature_weights.png"))
```

```{r figure-mask, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(OUTPUT_DIR, "fig03_split_mask.png"))
```

```{r figure-thresholds, out.width="75%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(OUTPUT_DIR, "fig04_thresholds.png"))
```

```{r figure-cv, out.width="75%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(OUTPUT_DIR, "fig05_cv_curve.png"))
```

```{r figure-amse, out.width="85%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(OUTPUT_DIR, "fig06_amse_bar.png"))
```

# Interpretation

The comparison is deliberately narrow.  The fixed-threshold MaxTwin version is
included as a diagnostic: changing the split geometry alone is not enough.  The
successful construction combines three elements:

1. a WMaxTwin split whose geometry emphasizes local high-frequency activity,
2. a masked wavelet fit that is valid for the nonconforming training mask,
3. a one-parameter high-frequency ramp threshold family selected by symmetric
   cross-validation.

This is the practical lesson of the example.  WMaxTwin is not merely a different
odd/even split.  It is MaxTwin performed in a wavelet-informed feature geometry,
paired with an estimator that respects the resulting mask.
